text: I arrive as UX researcher skeptical of synthetic users. My past: I value real user research and only trust synthetic methods when artifacts are inspectable and limitations are explicit.
My screen is Laptop 15 (1440x900, desktop).
Today I want to: Determine whether the method, dashboard, and evidence trail are credible.
My life goal is: Keep research decisions honest and avoid selling false confidence to teams.
I have 100 life-years. Each meaningful action costs 1 month(s); each committed dollar costs 1 year(s).

label: arrival
title: Betabots - Synthetic beta users with real product evidence
visibleText: Docs Contribute Betabots Cloud View Beta Run locally OPEN SOURCE Local-first Launch with a room full of ruthless beta users. Betabots runs truthful synthetic beta users in real browser sessions. Bots click, wait, think, doubt, compare notes, and leave inspectable evidence you can trust. View Beta Run locally Betabots Cloud View Beta Open the public dashboard demo. Browse sample Betabot reviews for GitHub, Self Degree, and this landing page in a deployed dashboard. Self Degree Education landing review: motivation, clarity, and conversion friction. Betabots Re

I think: I see strong credibility claims, but I need to inspect the dashboard evidence before I trust them.
My reaction: My reaction is cautiously interested; the promise is aligned with my work, but the landing copy is still doing more asserting than proving.
Idea: Put one concrete artifact preview on the first screen so I can judge evidence quality before opening the demo.
Life-cost decision: Spending 1 month of life is justified if the public demo shows whether this method keeps research decisions honest or just packages false confidence.
Truth assessment: My private judgment is that this is credible enough to inspect once, but not yet credible enough to recommend. Confidence: medium.
My impulse is to explore.
I think: Something about this moment makes me want to take one more step instead of leaving.
I follow a hunch and check /#how.

label: exploration
title: Betabots Demo Dashboard | Betabots
visibleText: Back to landing PUBLIC BETA DASHBOARD Three sample product reviews from cloud Betabots. Each review shows what a cohort noticed, what actions they took, and which findings would change a product decision. 162 cloud Betabots simulated across the sample pack GitHub github.com 82 Strong developer trust, mild onboarding sprawl. 60 bots 2-6 min sessions high-signal findings Top findings Open-source credibility is immediate, but new contributors split between docs, issues, and navigation. Bots trusted repository history faster than marketing claims. Mobile exploration stayed readable, but advanced flows became dense quickly. Observed actions Opened repository Read README Compared issues Checked co

I think: I am looking for whether these sample reviews expose enough evidence to judge the method, not just polished conclusions.
My reaction: My reaction is cautiously interested, but the dashboard still feels too summary-level for the trust it asks me to place in it.
Idea: Let me open one sample review into a full evidence trail with exact sessions, uncertainty, and decision impact separated.
Life-cost decision: Spending 1 more month is worth it only if the next action reveals inspectable evidence that helps me avoid false confidence.
Truth assessment: My honest judgment is that the product is credible-looking but not yet credible enough for research decisions; confidence 0.68.
My impulse is to continue.
I think: Your doubt has a clean place to land: method first, claims second. Take the same turn now.
I follow a hunch and check /#how.
I notice I am seeing the same kind of screen again, so my patience drops a little.
